title: Make yzn-open logging bounded or disableable
description:
yzn-open currently appends diagnostic events to `${YAZELIX_STATE_DIR}/logs/yzn-open.log` with no rotation or disable switch. This was useful for debugging Yazi-to-Helix opens, but it should not become an unbounded always-on log surface.

Acceptance criteria:
- Keep default diagnostics small and bounded, either with simple rotation/truncation or a clear opt-in debug mode.
- Provide a way to disable or minimize yzn-open logging without changing source code.
- Preserve enough failure evidence for Yazi open debugging when explicitly enabled.
- Keep the implementation simple and avoid introducing a logging framework unless the need becomes real.
close_reason: Implemented bounded yzn-open diagnostics: added YZN_OPEN_LOG levels off/error/info/debug, default info routing logs, debug-only payload/registry details, error-only failure logs, 64 KiB rotation with one yzn-open.log.1 backup, README/architecture docs, and tests for parsing, disable/error filtering, and rotation. Verified with cargo test, nix flake check, nix flake show, nix build, temp profile install, and nix profile upgrade.

title: Layer yzn Nushell user config
description: Allow yzn-scoped Nushell config layering without touching normal Nushell config. Packaged env/config should load first, then optional files under ~/.config/yazelix-next/nu via wrapper-generated runtime Nu source files.
close_reason: Implemented yzn-scoped Nushell config layering with packaged files first and optional user env/config files loaded from ~/.config/yazelix-next/nu via scripts/yzn-nu.sh.

title: Launch the Yazelix Zellij fork under Mars
description:
## Context
Extend the tiny Yazelix Next launcher so `yzn` opens Mars running the Yazelix Zellij fork instead of a plain shell. The fork is the temporary Yazelix KGP/APC passthrough Zellij source consumed by main Yazelix, not upstream nixpkgs Zellij.

## Decisions
- Keep this in the flake/package surface only.
- Consume the Yazelix Zellij fork as a non-flake source input.
- Do not add Home Manager, layouts, config generation, plugins, or pane policy in this slice.
- Preserve the per-language LOC scorecard.

## Acceptance Criteria
See the structured acceptance criteria field on this bead.
acceptance_criteria:
- `flake.nix` has a `yazelixZellij` non-flake input pointing at the Yazelix fork branch.
- `packages.${system}.yazelix_zellij` packages that fork from source using nixpkgs' Zellij packaging as the base where possible.
- `yzn` launches Mars with the packaged Yazelix Zellij fork as its command.
- `nix run` and `nix run .#yzn` still use the same launcher app surface.
- README says `yzn` opens Mars running the Yazelix Zellij fork.
- README documents `nix profile upgrade --refresh yazelix-next` as the installed-runtime update command.
- The per-language LOC scorecard is updated after the Nix and README changes.
- `nix flake show --all-systems` succeeds.
- `nix build .#yzn --no-link --print-build-logs` succeeds or any build blocker is recorded with the exact failing package/dependency.
- A temp profile install using `nix profile add --refresh /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix-next --profile <tmp>` exposes `<tmp>/bin/yzn`.
- The user's installed `yazelix-next` profile entry is refreshed after runtime changes.
- Human dogfooding confirms `yzn` opens a Mars window running the Yazelix Zellij fork before the bead is closed.
notes: Profile refresh evidence: ran `nix profile upgrade --refresh yazelix-next`; `nix profile list` now shows `yazelix-next` at `/nix/store/7imysps2q66y7aimv3xv6963hi6g3xhm-yzn`, and `/home/lucca/.nix-profile/bin/yzn` execs Mars with `/nix/store/3frryp6kl6sk7pqwcc0knksyf65411sa-zellij-0.44.3/bin/zellij`.
close_reason: Completed: yzn launches Mars with the packaged Yazelix Zellij fork; nix flake show, nix build .#yzn, temp profile install, zellij --version, profile refresh, and user dogfooding all passed.

title: Validate Mars path on macOS with Tyce
description:
## Why

Yazelix Next should keep Mars as the only terminal path until reality proves a second terminal is needed. Tyce is on macOS, so his test is the practical signal for whether Mars can carry the macOS story before the canonical Yazelix swap.

## Decision Context

The public swap gate now says Ghostty is postponed. Mars remains the default and only current terminal path. Ghostty becomes a narrow fallback only if Mars blocks macOS testing or swap readiness in a way that would slow the project.

Ratty remains future and non-blocking.

## Scope

In scope:
1. Decide when the core runtime is polished enough to ask Tyce to test it.
2. Give Tyce the smallest install/run instructions for macOS.
3. Capture whether Mars launches, renders, handles keyboard input, runs Zellij/Yazi/Helix, and opens files from Yazi into Helix.
4. Record whether failures are Mars bugs to fix or terminal-support pressure that justifies Ghostty.
5. Feed the result back into the public swap gate.

Out of scope:
1. Do not add Ghostty in this bead.
2. Do not add a broad terminal support matrix implementation.
3. Do not make Ratty part of the swap gate.
4. Do not require full public docs before trusted tester validation.

## Verification

Tyce can install or run the current Yazelix Next runtime on macOS and report whether the Mars path is usable for the core workflow. The result should be recorded in this bead or in the public swap gate.

## Stop Conditions

Stop if the core runtime is still too rough to produce useful external tester feedback. Stop if the observed issue is not macOS/Mars-specific and should be fixed in a normal core-runtime bead first.

title: Add Yazi sidebar pane
description: Add a minimal left Yazi pane to the packaged Zellij layout using packaged yazi, while keeping Yazi config layering out of scope unless needed.
close_reason: Implemented a minimal packaged Yazi sidebar: layout.kdl has a 20% left command pane using a Nix-substituted yazi path, main pane remains stacked, no Yazi config/user layering added. Verified flake show, build, built layout inspection, Yazi store reference, temp profile install, and profile refresh.

title: Add minimal Starship config surface
description:
## Why

Yazelix Next currently hardwires Starship into packaged Nushell config, while user prompt customization requires overriding Nushell prompt variables after packaged setup. That works but can create stacked prompt markers and does not give users a clean Starship-native way to tune the prompt. A minimal Starship config surface would let users visually customize the prompt while keeping Nushell layering and shell startup simple.

## Evidence

- `flake.nix:68`: packaged `nu/config.nu` is rendered with the packaged `starship` binary path.
- `flake.nix:80`: `yzn-nu` puts `starship` on `PATH` with the other shell tools.
- `nu/config.nu:4`: `PROMPT_COMMAND` always calls the packaged `starship prompt` command.
- `nu/config.nu:5`: packaged config clears `PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT` before user config is sourced.
- `runtime/yzn-nu.rs:27`: user Nushell config is read from `YAZELIX_NEXT_CONFIG_HOME/nu`.
- `runtime/yzn-nu.rs:77`: packaged Nushell config is sourced first, then optional user `config.nu` is sourced after it.

## Scope

In scope:
- Add a minimal Starship config path for managed `yzn` shells, likely `~/.config/yazelix-next/starship.toml` or `~/.config/yazelix-next/starship/config.toml`.
- Make packaged Nushell/Starship prompt use that Starship config when it exists.
- Keep packaged Starship defaults when the user config file does not exist.
- Document the Starship config path and its relationship to `nu/config.nu`.
- Add a focused contract check that proves the Starship config path is wired without requiring interactive shell rendering.
- Update `CHANGELOG.md` and the README LOC scorecard if implementation touches tracked files.
- Refresh installed `yzn` after runtime changes.

Out of scope:
- Do not add ratconfig, Home Manager, a prompt UI, or a broad settings file.
- Do not make `yzn` read the user's normal `~/.config/starship.toml` by default.
- Do not replace Nushell config layering or remove the existing `~/.config/yazelix-next/nu/config.nu` escape hatch.
- Do not design a theme/profile system yet.
- Do not hardcode the current turbofish smoke marker as a product default.

## Implementation Notes

1. Pick one path and document it before coding. Prefer the shortest path that matches existing config-root behavior.
2. Consider setting `STARSHIP_CONFIG` in the runtime Nushell environment when the user Starship config exists; otherwise leave Starship on packaged/default behavior.
3. Keep user `nu/config.nu` able to override prompt variables after packaged setup for advanced cases.
4. If a packaged default Starship config is added, keep it tiny and explicit.
5. Avoid TOML merging. Starship already owns its native config file format.

## Verification

- `nix flake check` should exit 0 and include any contract check changes.
- `nix build .#yzn --no-link --print-build-logs` should exit 0.
- `nix profile add --refresh /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix-next --profile <tmp>` should exit 0 for a clean profile install.
- `nix profile upgrade --refresh yazelix-next` should refresh the user's installed runtime.
- A non-interactive `yzn-nu --commands` smoke should prove that a temporary `YAZELIX_NEXT_CONFIG_HOME` with the Starship config file causes the runtime to point Starship at that file.
- Manual dogfooding, when requested, should show a visible prompt change in a fresh `yzn` window without stacked prompt indicators.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if Starship config support requires prompt rendering logic owned by Yazelix Next.
- Stop if the implementation needs to merge Starship TOML or infer Starship schema details.
- Stop if the cleanest solution conflicts with the existing user Nushell config layering contract.

## Maintenance Notes

- This is a native-file config surface like Mars, but narrower: Starship owns prompt formatting, Nushell owns shell startup, and `yzn` only chooses the config path.
- Rejected direction: using Nushell `PROMPT_INDICATOR` as the main prompt customization surface. It can stack with Starship and is better kept as an advanced user escape hatch.
notes: Implementation uses YAZELIX_NEXT_CONFIG_HOME/starship.toml when present and a readable empty fallback config otherwise, currently /dev/null, so normal ~/.config/starship.toml stays outside the managed runtime without adding a Nix fallback derivation.
close_reason: Implemented minimal Starship config surface: yzn-nu sets STARSHIP_CONFIG to YAZELIX_NEXT_CONFIG_HOME/starship.toml when present and to an empty packaged config otherwise; contracts cover user override and non-inherited fallback; README, ARCHITECTURE, CHANGELOG, and LOC scorecard updated; flake check/build/profile install/install smoke passed; installed yzn refreshed.

title: Add narrow Home Manager install surface
description:
## Why

A flake/profile install is useful for development, but some Yazelix users reasonably expect a declarative Home Manager owner. The canonical Yazelix swap should not rely only on imperative `nix profile` installation. The first Home Manager surface should stay narrow so it does not recreate main Yazelix's broad option/config machinery.

## User-Visible Behavior

A Home Manager user can enable Yazelix, get the final public `yzx` command in their profile, and get the Yazelix desktop entry. Rebuilding Home Manager should deterministically keep the selected package and desktop integration in place.

## Decision Context

The public swap gate records narrow Home Manager support as a hard canonical-swap blocker. Home Manager owns installation and desktop integration only for this slice, not runtime behavior or config management.

## Scope

In scope:
1. Add a minimal `homeManagerModules.default` or equivalent exported module.
2. Support `programs.yazelix.enable = true`.
3. Install the final Yazelix package as `yzx`.
4. Create the Yazelix desktop entry for the Mars-backed runtime.
5. Support a package override for local/dev/testing builds.
6. Document the minimal Home Manager usage.
7. Add focused checks for module evaluation and package/desktop-entry ownership.
8. Update README, ARCHITECTURE, CHANGELOG, and LOC scorecard when implemented.

Out of scope:
1. Do not manage full Yazelix runtime config declaratively.
2. Do not add terminal variants or extra terminal launchers.
3. Do not add ratconfig or config UI integration.
4. Do not manage Mars, Zellij, Yazi, Nushell, or Starship native configs through Home Manager in this slice.
5. Do not port main Yazelix Home Manager option parity.
6. Do not add migration logic from main Yazelix's existing module yet.

## Main Yazelix Context

Main Yazelix has a broad Home Manager module with terminal selection, extra launchers, config ownership, cursor config, Mars package/profile options, runtime tools, and generated runtime config activation. Yazelix Next should not port that wholesale. Use main as a reference for desktop-entry shape and package override ergonomics only.

Relevant main files:
1. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/home_manager/module.nix`
2. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/home_manager/options.nix`
3. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/home_manager/runtime_integration.nix`
4. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/flake.nix`

## Verification

1. Module evaluates in a minimal Home Manager config.
2. Enabling the module installs the configured Yazelix package.
3. Enabling the module creates the expected desktop entry.
4. Package override changes the installed package without changing the runtime contract.
5. No user runtime config files are generated by this first module.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text:
Package variant decision:

Expose both full and runtime-only variants from the flake, and let Home Manager consume either package rather than inventing a Home Manager-only runtime option first.

Preferred flake packages:

- `.#yzx`: full package with `yzx`, Mars, desktop entry, `yzx launch`, and `yzx enter`.
- `.#yzx-runtime`: runtime-only package with `yzx enter` and managed runtime tools, but no Mars and no desktop entry. If `yzx launch` exists in this package, it should print a clear message explaining that the full package is required for the Mars app.

Home Manager should initially expose package selection, not a separate `runtimeOnly = true` option. Example shape:

```nix
programs.yazelix.package = inputs.yazelix.packages.${pkgs.system}.yzx;
```

or:

```nix
programs.yazelix.package = inputs.yazelix.packages.${pkgs.system}.yzx-runtime;
```

A friendlier `programs.yazelix.variant = "full" | "runtime"` option can be added later if it proves useful, but the first slice should keep package ownership in the flake.

Support policy:

Mars remains the curated/default app path. Runtime-only is the escape hatch for users who want Kitty, Alacritty, Foot, Ghostty, WezTerm, or another terminal to run `yzx enter` as its initial command.

title: Design Nix-free installer and release bundle
description:
Goal: offer a Nix-free Yazelix install path after Yazelix Next is better shaped, without replacing Nix as the build/release system.

User-visible contract:
A user can install Yazelix with a curl-style installer, get a `yzx` command in their PATH, and run `yzx help`, `yzx enter`, and `yzx launch` without installing Nix. Nix remains the deterministic build/release factory that produces the bundle.

Recommended shape:
- Build release bundles with Nix.
- Installer detects OS/arch, downloads one prebuilt bundle, verifies it, extracts it under `~/.local/share/yazelix/versions/<version>`, updates `current`, and symlinks `~/.local/bin/yzx`.
- Bundle contains the runtime tools/assets: Mars, Yazelix Zellij fork, Helix fork, Yazi, Nushell, lazygit, starship, carapace, zoxide, plugins/WASMs/configs/assets.
- Add update/uninstall behavior only when the initial install path is stable.

Do not start this before the runtime shape is stable enough:
- command naming/swap plan is settled (`yzn` now, likely `yzx` at public swap)
- Mars/macOS has had contributor validation
- config surfaces are mostly settled
- popups/bar/tutor/keymaps are no longer moving quickly

First implementation slice when ready:
Prove the runtime can be relocatable by resolving binaries/configs relative to a bundle root instead of relying on embedded `/nix/store` paths. Keep this as a contract before writing the public installer script.

Rejected for now:
- Installing Nix from the installer: defeats the purpose of reducing adoption friction.
- Flatpak first: sandboxing and terminal/filesystem/agent behavior make it a worse first distribution target.
- One static binary: unrealistic because Yazelix is a multi-tool runtime bundle.

title: Ship owned Zellij config remap
description: Add a package-owned Zellij config and make yzn launch the Yazelix Zellij fork with it, starting by moving Zellij's native new-pane binding from Alt+n to Alt+m. This deliberately does not pull in the pane-orchestrator plugin yet; Yazelix-style workspace-terminal behavior remains a later slice.
acceptance_criteria:
config.kdl is a repo-owned KDL file.
yzn launches the Yazelix Zellij fork with --config pointing at the package-owned config.kdl.
Alt+n is unbound from the native new-pane default.
Alt+m runs the native Zellij NewPane action for now.
The installed package includes share/yazelix-next/config.kdl.
nix flake show, zellij setup --check with the config, nix build .#yzn, temp profile install, and profile refresh pass.
README LOC scorecard is updated.
close_reason: Completed: yzn packages and uses config.kdl; config unbinds Alt+n and binds Alt+m to the native Zellij NewPane action; zellij setup --check, nix flake show, nix build .#yzn, temp profile install, and live profile refresh passed.

title: Allow full native Mars config override
description:
## Why

Mars terminal preferences should be user-editable without adding a Yazelix Next settings schema or ratconfig surface. The current runtime always launches Mars with the packaged generated config, which keeps the slice reproducible but makes simple native Mars preferences like opacity, font size, window size, bells, cursor, colors, scrollback, and decorations require repository edits.

## Evidence

- `flake.nix:53`: the packaged Mars config is produced only from repo-owned `mars.toml` through `replaceVars`.
- `flake.nix:59`: `yzn-mars-config` installs that packaged config as `config.toml`.
- `flake.nix:186`: `yzn` always exports `MARS_CONFIG_HOME=${yznMarsConfig}` before launching Mars.
- `mars.toml:1`: the repo-owned file already contains native Mars top-level preferences.
- `mars.toml:21`: native Mars window preferences such as size, decorations, opacity, and opacity-cells are currently hardcoded in the package.
- `mars.toml:33`: native Mars font preferences are currently hardcoded in the package.
- `ARCHITECTURE.md:20`: `mars.toml` is currently documented as the terminal visual config owner.

## Scope

In scope:
- If `~/.config/yazelix-next/mars/config.toml` exists, launch Mars with `MARS_CONFIG_HOME` pointing at `~/.config/yazelix-next/mars`.
- Otherwise keep launching Mars with the packaged config output.
- Keep `yzn` launch command, Zellij command, environment, editor variables, and session state owned by the launcher.
- Document the user-owned Mars config path in `README.md` and the ownership boundary in `ARCHITECTURE.md`.
- Update `CHANGELOG.md` and the README LOC scorecard.
- Add or extend a focused contract check that verifies the fallback packaged path and user-config path selection behavior.
- Refresh the installed `yzn` runtime after changing the flake runtime.

Out of scope:
- Do not merge packaged and user Mars TOML.
- Do not validate or schema-check the user's full Mars TOML beyond relying on Mars itself to parse it.
- Do not introduce ratconfig, Home Manager, generated settings, or a CLI surface for Mars config.
- Do not expose individual Mars options as Nix options or Yazelix settings.
- Do not let Mars config own the `yzn -> Mars -> Zellij` startup command.

## Implementation Notes

1. Add a small launcher-level config selection in `yznCommand`: derive `YAZELIX_NEXT_CONFIG_HOME` from the existing config-root convention, defaulting to `~/.config/yazelix-next` when unset.
2. If `$YAZELIX_NEXT_CONFIG_HOME/mars/config.toml` exists, export `MARS_CONFIG_HOME=$YAZELIX_NEXT_CONFIG_HOME/mars`; otherwise export `MARS_CONFIG_HOME=${yznMarsConfig}`.
3. Keep the packaged config installed under `share/yazelix-next` if useful for inspection or copying.
4. Prefer shell-level existence checks in the existing `writeShellApplication` unless the launcher logic grows enough to justify a Rust helper.
5. Extend `checks/yzn-contracts.rs` only if it can test the behavior cheaply without launching Mars; otherwise add a small package-output/launcher-text check.

## Verification

- `nix flake check` should exit 0 and include the config-selection contract if implemented there.
- `nix build .#yzn --no-link --print-build-logs` should exit 0 and package the updated launcher.
- `nix profile add --refresh /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix-next --profile <tmp>` should exit 0 for a clean profile install.
- `nix profile upgrade --refresh yazelix-next` should refresh the user's installed runtime.
- Manual dogfooding, when requested, should show that adding `~/.config/yazelix-next/mars/config.toml` changes Mars terminal preferences while `yzn` still starts the managed Zellij runtime.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if Mars requires config merging to support this path; whole-file native ownership is the intended contract.
- Stop if a user Mars config can replace the `yzn` startup command or prevent Zellij launch through terminal TOML alone.
- Stop if the implementation needs a broad settings schema, ratconfig, or Home Manager surface.

## Maintenance Notes

- This is intentionally asymmetric with Zellij. Mars config is mostly terminal preference; Zellij config owns parts of the Yazelix runtime spine.
- Rejected direction: hardcoding a small list of Mars options in Yazelix Next. Native Mars TOML is the simpler and more complete user surface.
close_reason: Completed: yzn now uses a user native Mars config at YAZELIX_NEXT_CONFIG_HOME/mars/config.toml when present, falls back to the packaged Mars config, ships a packaged config copy for inspection, updates docs/changelog/LOC, verifies contracts/build/profile install, and refreshes the installed runtime.

title: Define Yazelix Next public swap gate
description:
## Why

Yazelix Next is moving fast as a clean architecture track, but making it public or swapping it into the main Yazelix identity has product, repository, support, and migration consequences. The main `luccahuguet/yazelix` repository should remain the canonical public project because it owns the older history, GitHub issues, links, social proof, stars, and public continuity. Before growing more features blindly, Yazelix Next needs a gate that says when it is good enough to become public-facing and how it can replace or coexist with main Yazelix without confusing users or stranding existing workflows.

## User-Visible Behavior To Distill

Users should understand which Yazelix they should install, what is supported, what is experimental, what is legacy, and what will keep working. Maintainers should know when Yazelix Next is ready to become the public default, and agents should know which repo/history/branch/support policy to preserve during the transition.

## Source Context

- Main Yazelix has history, issues, links, public users, stars, Home Manager surfaces, multiple terminal support, rich Yazi config management, `yzx tutor`, and many established workflows.
- Yazelix Next currently optimizes for the opposite shape: fewer lines, simpler architecture, one flake-installed `yzn`, Mars + Zellij + Yazi + Helix, narrow native config surfaces, and contract-backed slices.
- Some users need more than the current Next baseline. Example concern: Soder helped with main Yazelix and needed full Yazi config parsing plus Foot terminal support; Foot does not work on macOS, so terminal support needs platform/support-level policy rather than "support everything".
- `yzx tutor` is important and may be a release gate, because it teaches the mental model and reduces support load.
- Current open implementation beads include session attach/detach, Yazelix bar foundation, Zellij config sidecar, and Yazi config strategy. Those should be sequenced against this public-readiness gate.

## Scope

In scope:
- Define the public/swap readiness gate for Yazelix Next.
- Decide which repository strategy is preferred:
  - keep main Yazelix repo as canonical and replace its default implementation when ready,
  - keep Yazelix Next as a child/source repo consumed by main,
  - move old main implementation to a `legacy` branch and replace `main` contents,
  - or another explicit transition path.
- Decide what happens to Yazelix legacy after the swap: maintained, security/critical fixes only, frozen, archived, or branch-only.
- Define support levels for major feature families: terminal emulators, Yazi config, Helix/editor integration, Zellij/bar/session behavior, Home Manager/Nix, CLI commands, tutor, docs, and platform support.
- Specifically decide terminal support policy for Mars, Ghostty, Kitty, WezTerm, Foot, and user-preferred terminals.
- Decide whether `yzx tutor` or a minimal `yzn tutor` is mandatory before public default status.
- Identify which main Yazelix user-facing capabilities are required before swap, optional after swap, deliberately dropped, or legacy-only.
- Produce a concrete gate checklist and stop/go criteria that can be used before making Yazelix Next public or replacing main Yazelix.
- Record rejected migration strategies and support promises.

Out of scope:
- Do not perform the repository swap in this bead.
- Do not rewrite history, create branches, migrate issues, or alter GitHub metadata in this bead.
- Do not implement missing features such as tutor, terminal variants, Home Manager, Yazi config materialization, or bar widgets here.
- Do not promise broad terminal/config compatibility just because main Yazelix currently supports it.
- Do not create a release announcement until the gate is accepted.

## Key Questions

1. What is the canonical public repo after swap, and why should it remain `luccahuguet/yazelix`?
2. Does Yazelix Next become the code inside main Yazelix, or remain a child repo consumed by main?
3. What exact user workflows must work before public default status?
4. Which main Yazelix features are release-blocking versus legacy-support-only?
5. What support levels do we offer?
   - Tier 1: fully supported and tested
   - Tier 2: best-effort / maintained if simple
   - Tier 3: legacy-only / not in Next
   - unsupported: explicitly out
6. Which terminal emulators belong in each tier: Mars, Ghostty, Kitty, WezTerm, Foot, and custom host terminal paths?
7. Is `yzn` temporary, or does public Yazelix eventually return to `yzx`?
8. What does `yzx tutor` become in the Next world?
9. What is the migration story for existing users and Home Manager users?
10. What is the minimum public documentation surface before swap?

## Candidate Gate Shape

A first draft should evaluate these as possible release gates, not accept them blindly:

- Install/update: flake install and runtime refresh are reliable.
- Launch/session: `yzn` or future `yzx` can create, detach, reattach, and isolate sessions correctly.
- Core workflow: Mars/Zellij/Yazi/Helix open path works, including Yazi-to-Helix bridge reuse.
- UX: sidebar, stacked panes, bar/status, key hints, and desktop entry are coherent.
- Tutor/docs: a tutor or equivalent first-run guidance exists for the new mental model.
- Config: support levels for Mars, Zellij, Starship, Nushell, Yazi, and terminal variants are explicit.
- Migration: users know whether to stay on legacy, try Next, or switch; legacy support policy is explicit.
- Repository: main repo/branch strategy is chosen and reversible enough for safety.
- Support matrix: platforms and terminal variants are labeled honestly.
- Agent-readiness: AGENTS, Beads, contracts, checks, and docs give future agents enough context without chat history.

## Verification

- A Beads-backed decision exists with a public/swap gate checklist.
- The decision names required, optional, dropped, and legacy-only capabilities.
- The decision includes a terminal support matrix and platform notes.
- The decision includes a repo/history strategy for preserving main Yazelix continuity.
- The decision includes a tutor/onboarding stance.
- Follow-up implementation beads are created only after the user accepts the gate and chooses the next slice.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if this turns into a generic roadmap instead of a concrete go/no-go gate.
- Stop if the transition path would destroy or hide main Yazelix history, issues, stars, or links.
- Stop if support tiers are vague enough that users cannot tell whether a feature is supported.
- Stop if the plan requires supporting every main Yazelix feature before any public Next release; classify instead.
design:
Preferred swap strategy:

Keep luccahuguet/yazelix as the canonical public repository, but let the implementation start from the clean Yazelix Next codebase when the swap happens.

Planned sequence:

1. Keep building Yazelix Next as the clean lab until the irreducible core is clear.
2. Use this gate to classify work as release-blocking, later, legacy-only, or unsupported while that core is still being found.
3. Before the public swap, create a legacy or legacy-main branch from the current main Yazelix state.
4. Replace the implementation on main Yazelix with the Yazelix Next code in one explicit transition commit or a small reviewable commit series.
5. Keep old tags, releases, issues, links, stars, and git history reachable in the same GitHub repo.
6. Make yzx become the public command again after the swap. Keep yzn as the pre-swap/development command and possibly as a temporary alias during transition.
7. After the swap is proven, decide whether yazelix-next becomes archived, a mirror, or a continued development child repo.

This means the public repo does not start from a clean git history, but the implementation does start from a clean codebase.

Rejected migration paths:

Do not manually refactor the old main Yazelix implementation into Yazelix Next. That path preserves too much old architectural gravity, creates a long confusing deletion/refactor history, and gives future agents many stale patterns to copy back.

Do not force-push or replace main with an orphan clean-history branch by default. That would keep the GitHub repo identity but make local clones, contributor history, archaeology, and old links more hostile than necessary.

Do not keep legacy as an equal forever-product. It can remain available for existing users and critical fixes during transition, but the point of Yazelix Next is to stop carrying the full complexity burden forward.

Build-first with a gate:

The gate should not freeze development. We can keep building until the irreducible core is found, but the gate should steer decisions and prevent accidental promises. Every feature from main Yazelix should be classified before it is ported as release-blocking, useful after swap, legacy-only, or explicitly unsupported in Next. The goal is not feature parity. The goal is a smaller Yazelix that keeps the core user experience and drops or tiers the rest deliberately.

Initial support-tier bias:

Terminal support should start narrow. Broad terminal support is one of the easiest ways to recreate main Yazelix complexity. Tier 1 should start as Mars because it is the owned/default runtime. Tier 2 can include Ghostty only if it can be added without weakening the architecture. Kitty, WezTerm, Foot, and custom host-terminal paths should start as legacy or best-effort unless a specific contract proves their value and cost. Terminal-specific glue that requires broad config materialization, platform hacks, or compatibility shims should be unsupported without a clear user-visible contract.

Soder's Foot/Yazi-config needs are important context, but they should push us toward honest support tiers rather than a blanket support-everything promise. Foot not working on macOS is a good example of why platform support must be explicit.

Tutor bias:

yzx tutor is likely a public-swap gate. It teaches the Yazelix mental model and reduces support load. It does not have to block private Yazelix Next iteration, but it should probably block declaring Next the public default.
acceptance_criteria:
The final gate names the chosen repository strategy and preserves main Yazelix public continuity.

The final gate explicitly rejects manual refactor and orphan-history swap unless the user reverses this decision.

The final gate classifies main Yazelix capabilities as release-blocking, later, legacy-only, or unsupported.

The final gate includes support tiers for terminals and platforms.

The final gate states whether yzx tutor or an equivalent tutor is required before public default status.

The final gate says what happens to yzn, yzx, and the yazelix-next repo after the swap.
notes:
Terminal support decision:

Keep Mars as the only terminal path for now. Do not add Ghostty preemptively. The project should polish the Yazelix Next core first, then invite Tyce to test the Mars path on macOS once the runtime is coherent enough.

Ghostty is deferred, not rejected. It becomes the narrow fallback only if Mars blocks macOS testing or swap readiness in a way that would slow the whole project. If added later, Ghostty support should be a minimal launch/config path, not a port of main Yazelix's full terminal materialization and cursor stack.

Ratty remains an interesting future terminal path, but it is non-blocking and not part of the public swap gate.

Current terminal gate wording:

Canonical swap requires Mars as the intended default. macOS validation is required before swap, but the first macOS validation path is Mars. Ghostty is only introduced after Mars fails to satisfy that validation or creates too much schedule pressure.

Tutor decision:

yzx tutor, or an equivalent tutor restored under the final yzx command, is a hard canonical-swap blocker. It is not a blocker for private Yazelix Next iteration or Tyce testing.

The tutor should be implemented near the end, after keybindings and UI surfaces stabilize. In particular, defer it until the right sidebar or right popup direction, popup behavior, pane/sidebar layout, and keybinding map are stable enough that the tutor will not immediately go stale.

The Next tutor should stay extremely close to the one current yzx ships with. Main Yazelix source-of-truth references include rust_core/yazelix_core/src/front_door_commands.rs, rust_core/yazelix_core/src/tutor_document.rs, rust_core/yazelix_core/src/public_command_surface.rs, and docs/yzx_cli.md. Port behavior and structure deliberately, but update content for the new architecture and avoid dragging old runtime assumptions into Next.

Home Manager decision:

Narrow Home Manager support is a hard canonical-swap blocker. Flake/profile install is useful for development, but it is not the same user promise as a declarative Home Manager owner. The minimum Home Manager contract is installation and desktop integration only: enable Yazelix, install the final yzx package, create the desktop entry, support package override for local/dev testing, and default to Mars. Do not use the initial Home Manager surface to manage full runtime config, terminal variants, extra launchers, ratconfig, or generated native app configs.

Session decision:

Herdr-inspired attach/detach/reattach is not a hard canonical-swap blocker because main yzx does not have that polished story today. The gate only requires current launch/session isolation to be correct: plain launch behavior must be predictable, current Yazi-to-Helix opens must target the correct workspace/session, and the runtime must not leak opens into the wrong window. Rich Zellij session attach/reattach, session switching, a daemon, PTY ownership, socket API, and agent-aware session state are later work.

Bar decision:

The Yazelix bar, or a close standalone form of it, is a hard canonical-swap blocker because it is core to the Yazelix experience and communicates runtime state at a glance.

The widget gate is selective, not a parity port. Required before swap: session, editor, shell, term, codex_usage, cpu, and ram. Include claude_usage if it falls out of the same provider path as codex_usage without meaningful extra ownership. Defer workspace and opencode_go_usage unless they become trivial.

Workspace should not force session/workspace orchestration complexity into the first swap gate. OpenCode usage is optional because it has a different backing store and is less central to the current agent workflow.

Implementation bias: consume the standalone yazelix-zellij-bar foundation first, then add widgets one at a time behind checks. Keep the bar generic enough to live outside yazelix-next, and let yazelix-next own only integration, config, and wiring.
close_reason: Public swap gate documented: canonical repo strategy, Mars-first terminal policy, tutor/HM/bar/popup/config gate decisions, deferred session/right-sidebar scope, and follow-up implementation beads for config core and popups.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text:
Config CLI and ratconfig decision:

Yazelix Next should eventually support both CLI config commands and a ratconfig UI, but they must share one config core. The CLI is the non-negotiable base because it is scriptable, testable, agent-friendly, useful for Home Manager/debugging, and easy to cover with contracts. Ratconfig is the human-facing layer because it makes config discoverable inside Yazelix, especially from a popup.

Do not build separate CLI and UI logic. Preferred ownership is: one schema, one validation path, one migration/contract path, two frontends.

Ratconfig should absorb generic config mechanics, not Yazelix product policy. It may own reusable UI, TOML/JSONC patching, deterministic contracts, migrations, field rendering, diagnostics, config status rows, and helper APIs that Yazelix Next can consume. Yazelix Next owns which settings exist, what they mean, which native configs are safe, runtime policy, and safety guards.

Config surfaces should be tiered:

1. Semantic Yazelix config: edited by CLI and ratconfig UI, backed by ratconfig contracts/migrations. Examples include bar widgets, popup commands, sidebar width, and selected keybinding choices.
2. Native config surfaces: Mars TOML, Starship TOML, Nushell files, guarded Zellij KDL, and Yazi files. Users can edit these directly. Ratconfig may show status and open them, but should not blindly own their semantics. Curated TOML fields can be added later if they have clear value.
3. Guarded integration config: Zellij and Yazi can break the runtime spine, so Yazelix Next must keep validators and ownership boundaries. Ratconfig can render safe semantic fields and diagnostics, but must not become a generic native merge engine.

Avoid generic format translation unless tied to an explicit contract. Ratconfig is the config platform, not the config policy engine.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text:
Popup and right-sidebar decision:

Popups are a hard canonical-swap blocker. A right sidebar is not required for the swap.

Required popup surfaces before swap:

1. Config UI popup, for discoverability and editing Yazelix-owned settings.
2. LazyGit popup, for the daily project Git workflow.
3. Agent popup, for the agent-native Yazelix identity.
4. yzx menu popup, for command discovery and fallback launching.

Explicitly deferred:

1. Right sidebar.
2. Bottom/system information popup.
3. Zenith/bottom runtime monitor.

Uncertain but allowed if cheap:

1. LazyGit hook that refreshes Yazi git decorations after Git operations. Keep it only if it stays small and isolated; defer if it pulls in broad Yazi event/config machinery.
2. User-configurable popup registry. Add only if it falls naturally out of the popup owner as a tiny table of id, command, default key, and keep_alive. Defer if it requires broad schema migration, key collision handling, layout rewriting, or a complex validation UI.

Implementation bias:

Start with hardcoded first-party popups: config -> yzx config ui, git -> lazygit, agent -> codex, menu -> yzx menu. One popup runner should own launching, toggling, and focus behavior. Ratconfig can later edit the same popup table once the shape is stable.

Reasoning:

These popups cover customization, project workflow, agent workflow, and command discovery without forcing persistent right-sidebar orchestration. A right sidebar likely implies workspace/sidebar state, focus routing, layout policy, bar integration, and agent status ownership, so it belongs after the popup model is proven.

title: Explore Herdr-inspired agent-native runtime model
description:
## Why

Herdr is interesting because it treats the terminal runtime as agent-aware rather than just terminal text. It combines a real multiplexer core, persistent server/client sessions, PTY ownership, workspace/sidebar state, agent detection, socket/API automation, and integrations. Yazelix Next should study that direction before adding more isolated glue, because agent-native behavior could become a core differentiator if we can keep the architecture simple.

## User-Visible Behavior To Distill

A Yazelix Next window should eventually understand which panes are agents, what state they are in, and how that state rolls up into workspace/sidebar/status UI, while keeping real terminal processes and normal user tools intact. Agents should be runtime objects with metadata and automation surfaces, not just text patterns in panes.

## Herdr Ideas To Evaluate

- Persistent server/client model where closing the UI client does not kill pane processes.
- Real PTY ownership for panes, not fake rewritten agent views.
- Serializable workspace/tab/pane/layout/agent metadata separated from runtime objects like PTYs, child handles, readers, writers, and detectors.
- Agent detection through process names and terminal output by default, with socket/API integrations when available.
- State model such as blocked, working, done, idle, and rollups into a sidebar or bar.
- Live server replacement via PTY master file descriptor transfer with SCM_RIGHTS as a future inspiration, not immediate scope.
- libghostty-vt or equivalent terminal-state ownership as a possible long-term primitive.

## Scope

- Compare what Yazelix Next can get by staying on Zellij/Yazi/Helix plus small Rust helpers versus forking deeper versus building or adopting a multiplexer core.
- Identify the smallest agent-native vertical slice that fits the current `yzn -> Mars -> Zellij -> Yazi/Helix` runtime.
- Define which object should own agent identity, state, detection, lifecycle, and automation API in Yazelix Next.
- Decide whether an agent sidebar/bar should consume state from Zellij, from a Yazelix helper daemon, from pane-local detectors, or from future multiplexer ownership.
- Record rejected paths and why.

## Out Of Scope

- Do not build a terminal multiplexer in this bead.
- Do not replace Zellij or Mars as part of this bead.
- Do not add broad socket APIs, daemon state, SCM_RIGHTS handoff, or terminal emulation code without a later implementation bead.
- Do not treat text scraping alone as the final architecture if a cleaner integration surface exists.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Produce a recommendation for the next agent-native slice, ranked by simplicity, user value, and LOC impact.
- Name the surviving Herdr ideas and the ideas deliberately rejected for Yazelix Next.
- Define a minimal state model for agents if one is recommended.
- Identify concrete owners in this repo or external forks for any proposed runtime objects.
- Create follow-up implementation beads only after the user chooses a direction.

## Notes

The strongest architectural lesson is separation of serializable app state from runtime ownership. The immediate Yazelix Next question is not whether Herdr is impressive; it is how much of that agent-native model can be borrowed while preserving the project goal of minimal LOC and simple ownership.
notes:
Investigation 2026-06-25:

Herdr was cloned to /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix-related/herdr for local source inspection. Current upstream HEAD is 3c7327c fix: avoid pi release on internal session reloads (#800).

Herdr persistence is primarily server-owned runtime, not config replay. Running herdr starts or attaches to a background server; detaching closes only the client. The server keeps TerminalRuntimeRegistry objects, PTY ownership, readers/writers, detectors, layout state, sockets, and pane processes alive. Session JSON stores serializable shape such as workspaces, tabs, panes, cwd, labels, launch argv, and agent session refs; after a stopped server it restores shells and optional native agent sessions, not arbitrary live process handles.

Live process preservation across server replacement is the expensive part. Herdr live handoff duplicates PTY master fds, sends them to the replacement process with SCM_RIGHTS, imports them as runtime objects, waits for the new server to restore sockets, then old server drains/preserves runtimes and exits. This is a future inspiration only; it does not fit Yazelix Next near-term LOC or complexity goals.

Agent-native state is a clean layered model: TerminalState owns terminal identity, cwd, agent metadata, detected agent, fallback state, hook authority, custom status, and persisted agent session refs. PaneState only attaches view state to a terminal id. Detection is decoupled from the renderer and reads bottom-buffer/screen evidence through manifests. Hook/socket integrations can report stronger semantic state than text scraping. Workspace aggregation prioritizes blocked, unseen done, working, idle, unknown.

Main Yazelix already has a right agent sidebar as a managed terminal role, configurable through workspace.right_sidebar.* and launched by yzx agent. It does not yet have Herdr-style agent-state ownership: identity/state/automation are not durable runtime objects rolled up across sessions.

Yazelix Next currently launches zellij with --new-session-with-layout, so every yzn launch biases toward a fresh generated session. Zellij already has the first-order primitives that Herdr uses at the user level: named sessions, attach --create, list-sessions, kill/delete, detached background creation, on_force_close detach, and session serialization. That means the cheapest detach/attach improvement is to consume Zellij better, not build a server.

Mars Next context:

/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/mars-next is an experimental Ghostty-core terminal prototype, not production Mars. Its current architecture proves a terminal-core and renderer-neutral snapshot boundary first. The mars-next-live-session crate owns one PTY/process plus TerminalCore ordering through portable-pty: spawn, bounded PTY reads, queued writes, resize ordering, child exit, kill, and pull snapshots. It deliberately has no renderer/windowing dependency, no workspace/tab model, no background server/client split, no detach/attach, no socket/API automation, and no persistent multi-pane runtime. This makes Mars Next relevant as a future terminal-app primitive, but not as the immediate Yazelix Next session manager.

The Mars Next lesson aligns with Herdr on ownership separation: keep raw terminal/runtime handles behind a narrow owner and expose renderer-neutral state. The near-term Yazelix Next implication is the opposite of adopting Mars Next now: do not wait for a future Mars-owned PTY/session runtime to fix yzn sessions. Use Zellij's existing server/session primitives first, and keep Mars/Mars Next as the terminal window/core track.

Session slice implications for Yazelix Next:

Current yzn identity is top-level-launch-scoped, while detach/attach wants session-scoped identity. Reattaching to an existing Zellij session must not mint a fresh YAZELIX_HELIX_BRIDGE_SESSION_ID that disagrees with already-running Yazi and Helix panes. Before changing launch behavior, define whether plain yzn means attach/create a stable default Yazelix Next session or always create a new window/session. Layout should be applied when creating or resurrecting a session, not blindly on every attach. The first implementation check should prove command construction and bridge/session identity behavior without launching a GUI. Agent sidebar/state should stay deferred until this attach/detach contract is correct.

Recommendation ranked by simplicity and value:

1. First improve yzn session attach/detach around Zellij. Give yzn an explicit small session surface and stable default session behavior, likely using zellij attach --create plus the packaged layout/config, and add list/stop/delete only if the contract needs them. This directly fixes the user pain without owning PTYs.
2. Then add a tiny agent-state owner only after sessions feel good. The smallest state model should be unknown, idle, working, blocked, done-as-idle-unseen. Owner should probably be a small Yazelix Next helper or future bar input, keyed by Zellij session plus pane id. The sidebar/bar should consume that state; it should not own detection.
3. Defer Herdr-scale pieces: own multiplexer core, PTY ownership, libghostty-vt, socket automation, direct terminal attach, and SCM_RIGHTS live handoff. Revisit only if Zellij cannot support the desired session/mobile/agent workflows.

Rejected for now: replacing Zellij, forking deeper just for persistence, rolling our own terminal file manager or multiplexer, treating text scraping alone as the final agent-native architecture, or using Mars Next live-session as the current yzn detach/attach owner.
close_reason: Completed Herdr investigation: cloned and inspected Herdr, recorded persistence/session/agent-state findings, rejected near-term multiplexer/PTY ownership, and recommended the next Yazelix Next slice as Zellij-backed yzn attach/detach semantics before any agent sidebar/state work.

title: Add first-party popup runner surfaces
description:
## Why

The public swap gate classifies popups as a hard canonical-swap blocker. Yazelix Next needs first-party popup surfaces for config UI, LazyGit, an agent, and the yzx menu before it can become the public Yazelix default. A right sidebar is deliberately deferred, so popups are the near-term workspace extension model.

## User-Visible Behavior

A user can open and toggle four first-party popup surfaces from the managed Yazelix Next runtime:

1. Config UI popup, for discovering and editing Yazelix-owned settings.
2. LazyGit popup, for project Git workflow.
3. Agent popup, initially for Codex or the selected agent command.
4. yzx menu popup, for command discovery and fallback launching.

The popup behavior should feel like Yazelix: predictable keys, clear focus behavior, and no accidental interaction with the Yazi sidebar or stacked work pane layout.

## Source Context

- Public swap gate: `yazelix-next-public-swap-gate-o4i`.
- Current runtime: `yzn -> Mars -> Yazelix Zellij fork -> Yazi sidebar + stacked work panes`.
- Current key map uses `Alt Shift H` for the left Yazi sidebar and keeps `Alt m` for new panes.
- Main Yazelix has broader popup machinery, but Next should not port the full system blindly.
- Main reference files, for context only:
  1. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/rust_core/yazelix_core/src/workspace_commands/popup.rs`
  2. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/rust_core/yazelix_core/src/popup_runtime_command.rs`
  3. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/rust_core/yazelix_core/src/popup_session_facts.rs`
  4. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml`

## Scope

In scope:
- Define the smallest popup contract before coding.
- Add one popup runner owner for launching, toggling, and focus behavior.
- Start with hardcoded first-party popups: config, git, agent, and menu.
- Add or package the minimum tools required for those popups, including LazyGit and the selected agent command if needed.
- Pick stable default keybindings that fit the existing `Alt Shift` Yazelix-level layer.
- Preserve the Yazi sidebar, stacked pane layout, `Alt m`, and bottom Zellij key hints.
- Add focused checks for popup command/table construction and layout/config references without launching a GUI.
- Update `README.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `CHANGELOG.md`, and the LOC scorecard.
- Refresh the installed `yzn` runtime after runtime changes.

Out of scope:
- Do not add a right sidebar in this bead.
- Do not add a bottom/system information popup or Zenith monitor.
- Do not implement a broad user-configurable popup registry unless it falls naturally out of a tiny table and the user explicitly accepts it.
- Do not port main Yazelix's full popup/session/config machinery.
- Do not implement ratconfig UI here unless this bead is explicitly sequenced after the config core and the user chooses to combine them.
- Do not add LazyGit-to-Yazi git-decoration refresh unless it is clearly tiny and isolated.

## Implementation Notes

1. Prefer a tiny table of popup id, command argv, default key, and keep_alive only if that removes duplication without creating a registry project.
2. If a generic table becomes useful, keep it first-party and internal for this bead. User-configurable registry is a later decision.
3. Treat config UI as a placeholder command until the shared config CLI/ratconfig core exists, or sequence this bead after that config bead.
4. Agent popup should be agent-native but not require a right sidebar, agent status bar, or process detection.
5. Use main Yazelix only to understand proven behavior; do not copy large popup architecture without a current Next contract.

## Verification

- Contract check proves all required first-party popup ids exist and map to expected command argv/default keys.
- Contract check proves managed Zellij config/layout still contain the sidebar and stacked pane behavior.
- `nix flake check`.
- `nix build .#yzn --no-link --print-build-logs`.
- Temp profile install with `nix profile add --refresh /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix-next --profile <tmp>`.
- `nix profile upgrade --refresh yazelix-next` after runtime changes.
- Manual dogfooding, when requested: each popup opens, focuses/toggles predictably, and does not break Yazi-to-Helix opens.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if this turns into a general popup registry before the four first-party popups work.
- Stop if popup behavior requires right-sidebar orchestration.
- Stop if config UI popup cannot be meaningful before the shared config core exists; either sequence after the config bead or make the command an explicit placeholder.
- Stop if LazyGit refresh requires broad Yazi config/event machinery; defer that polish.

comments.author: lucca
comments.text: First slice implemented in yazelix-next: consume standalone yazelix-zellij-popup/yzpp as a flake input, configure one hardcoded LazyGit popup, and bind Alt Shift j to yzpp toggle. This intentionally does not close the bead: config UI, agent popup, yzx menu popup, popup registry, and close hooks remain out of scope for this checkpoint.

title: Reuse Helix editor pane from Yazi
description: Make the packaged Yazi opener keep Yazi as the sidebar when creating the first editor pane, then reuse a live Yazelix Helix bridge for later file opens. Keep this as a narrow opener script plus bridge-enabled yzn-hx; do not port main Yazelix pane orchestrator or full control plane.
notes: Final committed Rust helper shape: yzn-open keeps the bridge opener narrow with anyhow, serde, and serde_json only. Follow-up simplification removed fs-err and path-absolutize before commit; standard library filesystem/path APIs are enough for this Linux-only slice.
close_reason: Implemented with a Rust yzn-open helper. Yazi calls yzn-open; yzn-open reuses a live Yazelix Helix bridge via typed serde JSON over a Unix socket and falls back to opening yzn-hx in a right Zellij pane. Used anyhow, fs-err, path-absolutize, serde, and serde_json; skipped interprocess/duct/clap because they did not reduce this Linux-only slice enough. Verified cargo test, nix build, flake show, temp profile install, built Yazi debug, fallback smoke test, and refreshed installed yzn.

title: Verify yzn session and window isolation
description:
Check that separate yzn launches/windows are isolated from each other and do not accidentally share opener, Helix bridge, Zellij session, or Yazi state.

Context:
- yzn generates YAZELIX_HELIX_BRIDGE_SESSION_ID at top-level launch.
- yzn-hx defaults the bridge session id to yzn when the env is absent.
- yzn-yazi also defaults the bridge session id to yzn when the env is absent.
- yzn-open scans ${YAZELIX_STATE_DIR}/helix_bridge/<session_id>/ and uses registry zellij_session_name matching when present.
- Multiple Mars/Zellij windows or externally invoked helpers could expose isolation bugs if env propagation or fallback defaults are wrong.

Acceptance criteria:
- Document the expected isolation contract for one yzn window, multiple yzn windows, and helper commands invoked outside yzn.
- Verify whether yzn, yzn-yazi, yzn-hx, and yzn-open always agree on the intended session id inside a normal yzn window.
- Verify that opening files from Yazi in one yzn window cannot target a Helix bridge in another yzn window.
- Decide whether the yzn-yazi/yzn-hx fallback session id of yzn is acceptable or should fail/derive a safer id when launched outside yzn.
- Add the smallest focused tests or runtime inspections needed to prove the contract.
- Keep the README LOC scorecard updated if implementation changes files.
close_reason: Implemented and verified session/window isolation: documented the yzn bridge session contract, changed helper fallbacks away from shared literal yzn, propagated yzn-open's chosen session id when spawning editor panes, added Rust tests for fallback ids plus cross-yzn and cross-Zellij-session mismatch, and verified with cargo test, nix flake check, nix flake show, nix build, temp profile install, and nix profile upgrade.

title: Finalize the flake-installed yzn Mars launcher baseline
description:
## Context
Create the first Yazelix Next baseline as a Nix-compatible flake. The project should expose a short `yzn` command that opens Mars, support both `nix run` and profile installation from a local or absolute path, and keep a visible LOC scorecard so future work has a simplicity budget.

## Decisions
- Use Nix/Lix-compatible flakes as the project interface.
- Use `yzn` to avoid conflicting with the existing `yzx` command.
- Do not add Home Manager yet.
- Do not add Zellij or a workspace layer until the user explicitly chooses that next feature.
- Keep Mars packaging complexity isolated upstream.

## Acceptance Criteria
See the structured acceptance criteria field on this bead.
acceptance_criteria:
- `flake.nix` exposes `packages.${system}.yzn` and `packages.${system}.default` for supported Linux systems.
- `flake.nix` exposes `apps.${system}.yzn` and `apps.${system}.default` so `nix run` opens the launcher.
- The installed package provides `bin/yzn`, and `yzn` execs Mars without claiming the existing `yzx` command.
- README documents `nix run`, `nix run .#yzn`, absolute-path profile install with `--refresh`, and a per-language LOC scorecard.
- The LOC scorecard separates handwritten Markdown, handwritten Nix, and generated JSON lockfile lines.
- `nix flake show --all-systems` succeeds.
- `nix build .#yzn --no-link --print-build-logs` succeeds.
- A temp profile install using `nix profile add --refresh /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix-next --profile <tmp>` exposes `<tmp>/bin/yzn`.
- Human dogfooding confirms `yzn` opens a Mars window before the bead is closed.
close_reason: Completed by the staged baseline and superseding Zellij slice: flake exposes yzn/default apps and packages, README documents run/install/update and per-language LOC, nix flake show/build/temp profile install passed, and user confirmed yzn works.

title: Rebuild yzx tutor after keymaps stabilize
description:
## Why

The canonical Yazelix swap should not happen without `yzx tutor` or an equivalent tutor under the final public `yzx` command. The tutor teaches the workspace mental model and reduces support burden, but implementing it too early would make it stale while keybindings, right-sidebar or right-popup behavior, popups, and pane layout are still moving.

## Decision Context

The public swap gate records `yzx tutor` as a hard canonical-swap blocker, but not a blocker for private Yazelix Next iteration or Tyce macOS testing.

The user wants the Next tutor to be extremely close to the tutor current `yzx` ships with.

## Main Yazelix Sources Of Truth

1. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/rust_core/yazelix_core/src/front_door_commands.rs` owns current tutor command behavior and lesson content.
2. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/rust_core/yazelix_core/src/tutor_document.rs` owns Markdown parsing/rendering for tutor output.
3. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/rust_core/yazelix_core/src/public_command_surface.rs` owns `yzx tutor` command metadata.
4. `/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix/docs/yzx_cli.md` documents the public tutor surface.

## Scope

In scope:
1. Wait until keybindings and major UI surfaces are stable enough.
2. Implement `yzx tutor` behavior for the final public command surface.
3. Keep the tutor structure close to main Yazelix: root overview, begin, continue, list, workspace, discovery, troubleshooting, tool tutors, Helix tutor, and Nushell tutor, unless the user explicitly trims it.
4. Update tutor content for Yazelix Next realities: Mars, Zellij layout, Yazi sidebar, Helix bridge, popups, right sidebar or right popup, session behavior, config paths, and recovery paths.
5. Add focused contract checks that tutor entrypoints render and keybinding references match the current keymap owner.
6. Update docs, changelog, and LOC scorecard when implemented.

Out of scope:
1. Do not implement this before keymaps and UI surfaces stabilize.
2. Do not copy old runtime assumptions from main Yazelix.
3. Do not add progress persistence unless the user explicitly chooses it.
4. Do not make this block trusted tester feedback.

## Verification

1. `yzx tutor` or the pre-swap equivalent renders the guided overview.
2. Lesson entrypoints render without panics.
3. Tutor key hints come from the current keymap/config owner or are checked against it.
4. The final text teaches the actual Yazelix Next runtime, not old main Yazelix behavior.
5. The implementation stays close enough to main `yzx tutor` that returning users recognize the flow.
